note: Local-first context to build trust: present and historic facts you can cross-check with national agencies. Global scores come after.

AU.name: Australia
AU.trust_line: Start with what you can check locally — BOM, state emergency services, your own memory of recent summers.

present.title: El Niño teleconnection risk into 2026–27
present.detail: A strengthening El Niño (CPC: high odds of a very strong event by late 2026) typically raises odds of hotter, drier conditions across much of eastern and southern Australia in spring–summer — the pattern many Australians already recognise from past El Niño years.
present.verify: Compare with BOM climate outlooks and your state’s seasonal rainfall maps.

present.title: Extreme heat already in living memory (early 2026)
present.detail: Early 2026 saw extreme Australian heat, including reports of temperatures near 50°C under a heat-dome pattern — the kind of event households, grids, and fire agencies already treated as operational, not theoretical.
present.verify: Check BOM extreme heat records and your local council heatwave plans.

present.title: Bushfire readiness is seasonal, not optional
present.detail: Under drought-primed fuels and El Niño dryness risk, fire agencies treat high-attention seasons as the planning baseline. Smoke and evacuation pathways matter even if you are not in the bush.
present.verify: Your state CFA/RFS/DFES fire danger ratings and total-fire-ban notices.

historic.title: Black Summer 2019–20
historic.detail: Still the national reference for how fire, smoke, and health systems can be stressed together. Useful because most adults remember it — not abstract.
historic.verify: Royal Commission into National Natural Disaster Arrangements summaries.

historic.title: Past strong El Niño summers
historic.detail: Prior strong El Niño periods (e.g. 1997–98, 2015–16 class events) are the practical analogues Australians use when BOM outlooks turn dry/hot.
historic.verify: BOM ENSO wrap-ups and historical rainfall anomaly maps.

prep: Bookmark BOM warnings and your state emergency app
Water, shade, and medication plans for heatwaves
If in a bushfire zone: written leave-early plan, not only ‘wait and see’
Air quality: P2/N95 masks for smoke days

US.name: United States
US.trust_line: U.S. Drought Monitor, NIFC, and NWS products are public — check them against what you see outside.

present.title: Drought still widespread mid-2026
present.detail: As of mid-July 2026, roughly 39% of the U.S. (about 46% of the Lower 48) remained in drought, affecting on the order of 100+ million people. That is not a fringe reading — it is the Drought Monitor’s published map.
present.verify: droughtmonitor.unl.edu current map for your county.

present.title: Record-broad spring drought, elevated fire year
present.detail: Spring 2026 drought coverage hit record levels for the season in the Drought Monitor era. By late June, national wildfire acreage was well above the 10-year average (~157% in NIFC-class reporting).
present.verify: NIFC national situation reports; your state forestry page.

present.title: El Niño amplifies forward heat/drought odds in many regions
present.detail: CPC’s path toward a very strong late-2026 El Niño does not guarantee extremes everywhere, but it tilts seasonal odds — the same language NWS seasonal outlooks use.
present.verify: CPC 3-month outlook maps for temperature and precipitation.

historic.title: 2011–2012 and 2021–2022 drought/fire seasons
historic.detail: Recent multi-region drought and western fire seasons are the lived baseline many Americans use to judge whether ‘this year feels different.’

historic.title: Strong El Niño winters (1997–98, 2015–16)
historic.detail: Classic U.S. teleconnection patterns (wetter South, different West outcomes) are documented in CPC archives — useful for calibrating expectations, not destiny.

prep: Know your county’s drought stage and water restrictions
Heat: cooling access plan; check on vulnerable neighbours
Wildland–urban interface: defensible space and go-bag
Follow NWS alerts, not only social media

NZ.name: New Zealand
NZ.trust_line: GeoNet and MetService are the ground truth most Kiwis already use.

present.title: Volcanic / submarine unrest is a live local channel
present.detail: Mid-July 2026 submarine volcanic activity triggered tsunami warnings that were later downgraded — a reminder that NZ’s risk is often minutes-to-hours, not abstract global scores.
present.verify: geonet.org.nz alerts and your local tsunami zone maps.

present.title: Seismic exposure is permanent
present.detail: Alpine Fault and Hikurangi scenarios are national planning fixtures. Global M7+ years matter less than whether your building and household are ready.
present.verify: GeoNet hazard maps; local council civil defence.

historic.title: Christchurch 2010–11 and Kaikōura 2016
historic.detail: The national memory of what earthquakes actually do to infrastructure and insurance — still the best trust anchor for why prep is local.

prep: Drop, cover, hold; 7-day household kit where practical
Know tsunami blue lines if coastal
Follow GeoNet and Civil Defence, not rumour chains

PH.name: Philippines
PH.trust_line: PHIVOLCS and PAGASA are the agencies whose alerts you can cross-check against what communities felt.

present.title: M7.8 Mindanao (7 June 2026)
present.detail: The strongest quake of 2026 globally so far produced violent shaking (up to intensity IX class reporting) and tsunami monitoring — a concrete, recent event many Filipinos can place on a map.
present.verify: PHIVOLCS event page and intensity reports for your province.

present.title: Typhoon + ENSO interaction ahead
present.detail: El Niño years often change rainfall and tropical cyclone behaviour in the western Pacific. Treat seasonal PAGASA outlooks as the planning layer, not social-media storm rumours.

historic.title: Bohol 2013, Mindanao sequences, Taal unrest
historic.detail: National memory of quakes and volcanoes is the reason household kits and evacuation drills are not ‘globalist’ ideas — they are local experience.

prep: PHIVOLCS intensity scale literacy
Go-bag, family meeting point, offline maps
Official SMS/alert channels over forwarded chats

UK.name: United Kingdom
UK.trust_line: Met Office warnings and Environment Agency flood alerts are what you can verify against the weather outside your window.

present.title: Heat, flood, and infrastructure stress are the practical UK risks
present.detail: UK exposure is less about megathrust quakes and more about heatwaves, surface-water flooding, and cascading infrastructure strain — already visible in recent summers.
present.verify: Met Office warnings map; local flood risk postcode search.

present.title: El Niño’s UK signal is subtler but real
present.detail: ENSO teleconnections to the UK are weaker and noisier than for Australia or the U.S. South — do not expect a one-to-one ‘El Niño = X’, but do watch Met Office seasonal outlooks as the event strengthens.

historic.title: 2022 heatwave and recent named storms
historic.detail: Living memory of 40°C-class heat and winter storm disruption is the trust base for why heat plans and flood kits are local, not abstract.

prep: Heatwave plan for vulnerable people
Know your flood risk and insurance gaps
Met Office app alerts over social media chains

ZA.name: South Africa
ZA.trust_line: SAWS outlooks and municipal water notices are checkable against heat, fire, and taps.

present.title: El Niño points to a hotter, drier summer risk inland
present.detail: A strengthening El Niño into late 2026–early 2027 raises odds of above-average heat and below-average rain over much of the summer rainfall region (e.g. Gauteng, Limpopo) — a pattern South Africans have lived through before.
present.verify: SAWS seasonal climate watch; local municipality water restrictions.

present.title: Early-2026 fire stress already demonstrated
present.detail: Severe fire activity in early 2026 under warm/dry conditions is part of the present baseline, not only a future scenario.

historic.title: Cape Town Day Zero era and prior El Niño droughts
historic.detail: Water scarcity memory is one of the strongest trust anchors in SA risk communication — people know what empty dams feel like.

prep: Household water buffer where safe/legal
Heat and fire readiness for the dry season
Follow SAWS and municipal alerts

CL.name: Chile
CL.trust_line: SENAPRED and SHOA alerts are the local channel; coastal and fire risk is lived experience.

present.title: Early-2026 coastal fires with fatalities
present.detail: January–February 2026 coastal fires caused reported deaths and community destruction — a present-tense event, not a model output.
present.verify: SENAPRED situation reports and local municipality notices.

present.title: Seismic and tsunami culture is local knowledge
present.detail: Chile’s earthquake and tsunami preparedness is among the world’s most practised. Global scores matter less than whether your household still treats drills as real.

historic.title: 2010 Maule and major fire seasons
historic.detail: National memory of mega-quakes and destructive fires is why prep messaging lands — it matches biography, not theory.
historic.verify: Official anniversary and hazard education pages.

prep: Tsunami zone awareness if coastal
Fire defensible space in wildland interface
Official alert apps over forwarded videos

GLOBAL.name: Global / other region
GLOBAL.trust_line: If your country is not listed yet, use national met and geological services as your first source — this site is a map, not a substitute.

present.title: Two structural drivers in 2026
present.detail: Nuclear risk after New START expiry, and a strengthening El Niño with CPC assigning high odds of a very strong late-2026 peak. Local impacts still depend on where you live.
present.verify: Your national weather service seasonal outlook + your civil-protection agency.

historic.title: Use national archives first
historic.detail: Trust grows when global dashboards point you back to agencies you already know — not when they replace them.
historic.verify: Bookmark your country’s official warning portal.

prep: Identify your national warning service
Household water/food/meds buffer scaled to local hazards
Prefer primary sources over viral posts
